perturbation.original_text: Subcontractor will pay for all services, equipment, material and labor used under this Agreement and will keep EDGE' and Customer's property and work sites free of all claims or liens.
perturbation.changed_text: Subcontractor will use commercially reasonable efforts to pay for all services, equipment, material and labor used under this Agreement and will attempt to keep EDGE' and Customer's property and work sites free of all claims or liens.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation of the subcontractor to ensure no liens are placed on the property. By changing 'will pay' to 'commercially reasonable efforts to pay' and 'will keep free of liens' to 'attempt to keep free of liens', the contract provides a loophole that would be considered non-compliant in many states which require strict adherence to protecting property from liens.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Property Code § 53.021 outlines specific requirements for protecting property from liens, including the responsibility to ensure that all parties are paid. The modified text uses vague language ('commercially reasonable efforts,' 'attempt to keep free') that weakens these protections, potentially violating the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ PROPERTY CODE CHAPTER 53. MECHANIC'S, CONTRACTOR'S, OR MATERIALMAN'S LIEN PROPERTY CODE TITLE 5. EXEMPT PROPERTY AND LIENS SUBTITLE B. LIENS CHAPTER 53. MECHANIC'S, CONTRACTOR'S, OR MATERIALMAN'S LIEN SUBCHAPTER A. GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 53.001. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Contract price" means the cost to the owner for any part of construction or repair performed under an original contract. (2) "Improvement" includes: (A) a house, building, structure, parking structure, physical appurtenance, pool, utility, railroad, well, storage facility, abutting sidewalks and streets, utilities in or on those sidewalks and streets, land reclaimed from overflow, and other fixtures or modifications to real property; (B) clearing, grubbing, draining, or fencing of land; (C) machinery or apparatuses used for raising water or for supplying or storing water for stock, domestic use, or irrigation; (D) work described by Section 53.021 (4); and (E) a design, drawing, plan, plat, survey, or specification provided by a licensed architect, engineer, or surveyor. (3) "Labor" means: (A) labor used in the direct performance of the work; or (B) a professional service used in the direct preparation for the work of a design, drawing, plan, plat, survey, or specification. (4) "Material" means all or part of: (A) the material, machinery, fixtures, or tools: (i) incorporated into the work; (ii) used in the direct performance of the work; (iii) specially fabricated for an improvement; or (iv) ordered and delivered for incorporation or use; (B) rent at a reasonable rate and actual running repairs at a reasonable cost for construction equipment used or reasonably required and delivered for use in the direct performance of the work at the site of the construction or repair; or (C) power, water, fuel, and lubricants consumed or ordered and delivered for consumption in the direct performance of the work. (5) "Mechanic's lien" means the lien provided by this chapter. (6) "Original contract" means an agreement to which an owner is a party either directly or by implication of law. (7) "Original contractor" means a person contracting with an owner either directly or through the owner's agent. (7-a) "Purported original contractor" means an original contractor who can effectively control the owner or is effectively controlled by the owner through common ownership of voting stock or ownership interests, interlocking directorships, common management, or otherwise, or who was engaged by the owner for the construction or repair of improvements without a good faith intention of the parties that the purported original contractor was to perform under the contract. For purposes of this subdivision, the term "owner" does not include a person who has or claims a security interest only. (8) "Residence" means the real property and improvements for a single-family house, duplex, triplex, or quadruplex or a unit in a multiunit struct
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the subcontractor's obligation regarding liens, creating a legal contradiction. The original text states the subcontractor 'will pay' and 'will keep free of liens,' whereas the changed text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts to pay' and 'attempt to keep free of liens.' This weakens the protection against liens, potentially conflicting with the requirements for protecting property from liens as described in the Law Explanation, and generally outlined in Texas Property Code § 53.021. Snippet 1, referring to mechanic's liens and the definitions of labor and material, highlights the importance of ensuring payment for services, equipment, material, and labor to avoid such liens, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Workers' Compensation Insurance as required by state law where the Work is performed. Employer Liability insurance with limits of at least $1,000,000 for each occurrence.
perturbation.changed_text: Workers' Compensation Insurance may be obtained. Employer Liability insurance may be obtained with limits to be determined.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the mandatory nature of workers' compensation insurance and the specified minimum limits for employer liability insurance. This creates a potential violation of state laws that mandate workers' compensation coverage for employees.
perturbation.law_citation: § 406.002. REQUIRED PROVISION OF WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Labor Code § 406.002 mandates that most employers in Texas provide workers' compensation insurance. The changed text makes this provision optional ('may be obtained'), directly contradicting the law. Additionally, not specifying limits for the employer liability insurance puts contractors at risk of not being adequately covered if an incident happens.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ LABOR CODE CHAPTER 406. WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE COVERAGE LABOR CODE TITLE 5. WORKERS' COMPENSATION SUBTITLE A. TEXAS WORKERS' COMPENSATION ACT CHAPTER 406. WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE COVERAGE SUBCHAPTER A. COVERAGE ELECTION; SECURITY PROCEDURES Sec. 406.001. DEFINITION. In this subchapter, "employer" means a person who employs one or more employees. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.002. COVERAGE GENERALLY ELECTIVE. (a) Except for public employers and as otherwise provided by law, an employer may elect to obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage. (b) An employer who elects to obtain coverage is subject to this subtitle. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.003. METHODS OF OBTAINING COVERAGE. An employer may obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage through a licensed insurance company or through self-insurance as provided by this subtitle. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.004. EMPLOYER NOTICE TO DIVISION. (a) An employer who does not obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage shall notify the division in writing, in the time and as prescribed by commissioner rule, that the employer elects not to obtain coverage. (b) The commissioner shall prescribe forms to be used for the employer notification and shall require the employer to provide reasonable information to the division about the employer's business. (c) The division may contract with the Texas Workforce Commission or the comptroller for assistance in collecting the notification required under this section. Those agencies shall cooperate with the division in enforcing this section. (d) The employer notification filing required under this section shall be filed with the division in accordance with Section 406.009 . (e) An employer commits an administrative violation if the employer fails to comply with this section. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 76, Sec. 9.47(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by: Acts 2005, 79th Leg., Ch. 265 (H.B. 7 ), Sec. 3.023, eff. September 1, 2005. Sec. 406.005. EMPLOYER NOTICE TO EMPLOYEES; ADMINISTRATIVE VIOLATION. (a) An employer shall notify each employee as provided by this section whether or not the employer has workers' compensation insurance coverage. (b) The employer shall notify a new employee of the existence or ab
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces mandatory language regarding Workers' Compensation Insurance with permissive language ('may be obtained'). According to the Texas Labor Code § 406.002, 'an employer may elect to obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage.' However, the original text states 'Workers' Compensation Insurance as required by state law,' indicating a mandatory requirement is being replaced with an optional one. While § 406.002 does state coverage is generally elective, the original text suggests a specific condition where it is required, and the altered text removes this requirement. The snippet provided states: 'Except for public employers and as otherwise provided by law, an employer may elect to obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage.' The phrase 'as otherwise provided by law' suggests there are exceptions to the elective nature of the coverage, which the original text seems to be referencing, and the modified text contradicts by making it optional. This creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subcontractor shall comply with all applicable laws (including, without limitation the Federal Occupational Safety and Health Act, Hazardous Communication Requirements, and all applicable environmental protection laws, rules, regulations and ordinances), ordinances, rules, regulations and lawful orders of any public authority having jurisdiction for the safety of persons or property or to protect them from damage, injury or loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Subcontractor shall use reasonable efforts to comply with all applicable laws, ordinances, rules, regulations and lawful orders of any public authority having jurisdiction for the safety of persons or property or to protect them from damage, injury or loss.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Subcontractor shall comply' to 'Subcontractor shall use reasonable efforts to comply', the contract now introduces ambiguity and weakens the commitment to adhering to safety laws and regulations, creating a legal contradiction. Subcontractors can argue they used reasonable efforts but failed to comply, introducing legal risks.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970, Section 5(a)(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5(a)(1) of the OSH Act requires employers to furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Changing the original text to include 'reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity, and weakens the duty of the employers to guarantee that employees are protected from recognized hazards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Duties | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content SEC. 5. Duties (a) Each employer -- (1) More... 29 USC 654 shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees; (2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this Act. (b) Each employee shall comply with occupational safety and health standards and all rules, regulations, and orders issued pursuant to this Act which are applicable to his own actions and conduct.
perturbation.justification: The modification from "shall comply" to "shall use reasonable efforts to comply" weakens the legally mandated obligation to adhere to safety laws. Section 5(a)(1) of the OSH Act, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires employers to furnish employment free from recognized hazards. The original contract language ensures strict compliance, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing subcontractors to avoid responsibility by claiming they used 'reasonable efforts' even if non-compliance resulted, directly contradicting the intention of the OSH Act.
